content:
Hey there! My name is Anirudh. I’m a recent graduate in Computer Engineering from the University of Mumbai.
My research interests lie in the fields of Computer Graphics and Artificial intelligence particularly encompassing the recent advancements in virtual environments, simulations, physical animations, realtime rendering and reinforcement learning. I also draw stuff every now and then, either through a physical medium or through code and doodle around on my guitar for hours at end.
I will soon begin my graduate studies in Computer Science at the Georgia Institute of Technology in Fall, 2021.

title: Anirudh Mukherjee

content:
Yes, it’s over.   Man, what a year. I could go ahead and give you an analogy that paints 2020 alongside some situation that goes absolutely bonkers, but I shall refrain from doing so. We all know how it went. The whole year (or at least most of it) were some of the most trying times we might have faced in recent memory. I don’t think I have personally witnessed any such event in my lifetime given that I’m still considerably young (but not for long, haha…) which had such a massive impact worldwide. And as I am writing this, the virus still rages on. Although this time, there seems to be a glimmer of light shining at the end of this dark tunnel. That’s the phase we are in now, I call this the hope phase. Throughout our journey through this tunnel we’ve all had our own personal struggles, some caused by the ongoing pandemic and some catalyzed by it. If you’ve managed to see the light of this day, congratulations. You’ve come out stronger. And as we all have been saying, you survived 2020.
   Your current location.   Before I go on rambling about this year, I must say that every single word jotted down over here comes from the point of view of a very privileged individual. The year was way worse.
If I had one word to describe this year, it would be uncertainty. As the world locked itself inside, a large number of questions started popping up in everyone’s minds. Questions that did not have a clear answer back then. These were uncharted territories we were venturing out into, something we were never trained for. Something that was as easy as staying indoors but something that was as challenging as, well, staying indoors. We went inside without a clear idea of when it would be possible to come out again. And that’s when anxiety slapped us hard. We had no clear idea about our future, everyone’s plans were suddenly hacked and slashed into pieces. We were worried about our livelihoods, layoffs, education, economy, viral tiktoks, the American elections and lastly… the health of ours and our loved ones. We were distanced. The future was virtual. Virtual meets, virtual stand ups, virtual movie time, virtual classrooms, just take whatever you used to do before the pandemic happened and add the word virtual before it and voila, you’re in the future. Future was the key player looming over everyone’s heads while they had to cope with another important player in the game, the present. And boy, coping was hard.
  How I cope with things, which is okay.   I was in my final year of engineering when the unspeakable happened - traditional day at college. Coincidentally it was also my birthday. Also coincidentally, it was my last day at college before they were closed off. So yeah signing off on a very auspicious day, I was naive enough to think this would be easy back then. I was super happy to just sit at home and wait for this virus thing to pass. Two days later, I wore a mask for the first time in my life as I went to visit my significant other after her exams (which were called off midway). It was indeed very weird as very few people outside donned a mask, not to say that I looked like an absolute joker because I was wearing it upside down. But then in a week or so, a complete lockdown order had been issued for the entire country. Everyone was home. Just, passing time. Just need to pass some more time. Okay we’re almost there. Nope, some more time. And so it went on. There was no end.
  A pictorial representation of my thoughts.   I had just been accepted to my top choice of graduate school and this is exactly when my worries began. I could not see a solution to our problem in the near time and I did not want to risk beginning my studies in the middle of a pandemic. More importantly I did not want to leave this way, without saying proper goodbyes to my loved ones. I was worried left, right and center about how things would pan out. Eventually my university let us all defer our admissions by one year and I am so glad and thankful to them for being this considerate towards their prospective students. Even the American elections raised my BP through the roof but I won’t comment on that. Indoor exercises, art, programming, video games, books, making this damn website and mindlessly wandering around on the internet kept me busy most of the time. But every few weeks or so I would break down. And if you did too, that’s okay. It’s completely normal. Getting back up was another recurring thing that I did this year since I was being knocked down quite too often for my own taste. Which is my cue to insert this very motivational quote said by one of my favourite boxers of all time. Okay, jokes aside, it is definitely relevant. Sorry if the picture is too much of what you see on your virtual family groups.
  What a guy, eh?   Every so often something or the other would creep up in my mind. Something twisted. The past, the future or the fact that I still hadn’t learned how to make a proper roti. Sitting alone by yourself does that to you. All forms of thoughts come and go. Insidious ones, quite often if you’re like me. All the free time I had got me to speculate all the different possible scenarios for my future, just like Dr. Strange did in that movie. Right down to the point where I started questioning my own sanity. This was not good for my mind, so I started meditating, writing down my thoughts, laughing at how stupid my thoughts were, eliminating stressors through lists and talking besides the million other things I got myself into to keep myself sane. I barely saw any new faces for a couple of months. I missed people. I missed my partner the most. We would video call each other at nights and that would be the happiest moment of my day. So yeah, it was difficult. There were a lot many challenges this year that were completely new to me and got me by surprise.
This was a year of uncertainty. Solidarity in uncertainty. Hope in uncertainty. Acceptance in uncertainty. And perseverance in uncertainty. This was the year I got to learn how to face my biggest fear- uncertainty itself. Not that I am a black belt at it yet, but I did learn a lot. This was a year of reflectance for all of us, for I am sure we must have had those days when we sat down and had a good hard look at ourselves and our very own lives. This was a year of courage. All of us deserve to give ourselves a pat on our backs because no matter how low things got this year, we pushed through. And we made it. If you’re still struggling through some rough tides, hang in there buddy. Things will get better. Remember, one day at a time.
Welcome to 2021. Have an amazing year ahead.
:)
summary: Yes, it’s over.   Man, what a year. I could go ahead and give you an analogy that paints 2020 alongside some situation that goes absolutely bonkers, but I shall refrain from doing so.
title: About 2020.

content:
You might have heard of the term 'shading' when pertaining to pencil sketches. Or maybe used the term a 'shade' of red when referring to a particular colour. Well, in the computer world shaders are programs that run parallely on your GPU and determine how stuff gets drawn onto your screen. An interesting characteristic of a shader program is that it runs for every single pixel on the screen. To make it a bit easier to grasp, we can think of them as tiny little programs that help us in drawing all sorts of things on a screen with ease, which we shall see in a moment. How do they work? Now you might be thinking, why does this shader thing run on our GPUs? Why not make them work on CPUs like most normal programs do? That has something to do with what I had said earlier regarding the way these things work. These tiny little programs run for every single pixel on your screen. So if you’re using a 1080x720 monitor, the shader runs 777,600 times. But wait, that’s not such a large number in terms of computing things, right? Yes but these shader programs run every single  frame for every single pixel since they need to keep rendering something on the screen based on your monitor’s refresh rate. Which means it has to perform those 777,600 calculations every single frame and if we’re expecting a performance of at least 30 frames per second. That’s 23,328,000 calculations every second. Which is obviously insane. This is where the parallel processing power of a GPU comes in. You can think of it as a large chunk of cheese passing through a surface with multiple holes in it, like a cheese grater as compared to passing through a surface with a single hole in it, which would be the case of a CPU. Because of this parallel architecture which makes use of a large number of tiny microprocessors, all the 777,600 calculations can easily pass through it 30 times over in a second. One more important thing to remember is that since these shader programs run in parallel, each individual pixel has no idea what the other pixel is up to which means that each thread of the program is clueless about what’s going on with the other threads. This means you cannot check for the state of the (n+1)th or the (n-1)th pixel which some of you might have done before. Also with each new wave in every frame, the GPU gets filled in with new information which makes it impossible to know what it was doing before. The parallel computation capability of shaders makes it a very powerful tool but it is this very same power that translates into its notorious complexity. Types and Flavours There are a number of shading languages out there with GLSL (OpenGL Shading Language) and Cg/HLSL (High Level Shading Language) being some of the most popular ones. Based on what the shader program does, we usually classify them into two types-
 Vertex Shaders and Fragment Shaders  A Vertex shader, as the name suggests, takes in positions of certain objects that are to be drawn and converts them into the appropriate 3D coordinates for your screen whereas the Fragment shader is responsible to determine the final colour of each individual pixel on the screen. We’ll have a look about these in depth in a future post. Again there are a number of steps before the shaders are invoked which we’ll go through sometime in the future. Or if you’re adventurous enough you can have a look at the rasterization pipeline for yourself.There’s one final concept to note before we move on to actually seeing a shader program and that is a uniform. Uniforms are inputs to the shader program from the CPU.These uniforms cannot be modified and are sent by the CPU to all threads running on the GPU. Hence, all the inputs from the CPU are the same for all threads from where we get the name ‘uniform’. Some common uniforms passed on to these programs are the screen resolution, mouse position and time.

Okay then, enough talk. Let’s have a look at some code.
#ifdef GL_ES precision mediump float; #endif uniform vec2 u_resolution; uniform vec2 u_mouse; uniform float u_time; void main() { gl_FragColor = vec4(1.0,0.0,0.0,1.0); }    The result on your screen    This is the simplest possible code for a fragment shader written in GLSL. We’ll go through it from top to bottom. The lines between ifdef and endif sets the precision value of a float to medium precision if GL_ES is defined, which is true in the case of mobile and browsers. We can go for lowp or highp precisions for lower and higher precisions respectively if we wish to do so.
Next are the uniforms which are passed into the shader program. They are of the type vec2 which is a 2 dimensional vector of floats which is basically a group of two float values (x,y). You can think of them as tuples or just a group of values. We also have vec3 and vec4 types which is basically a group of 3 floats and a group of 4 floats.
Now we come to the main function. The gl_FragColor is a reserved variable which outputs the final color onto the screen which in this case is a vec4 having the red, green, blue and alpha channels as its arguments. The channel values are floats that range between 0 and 1. Hence, the red is set to 1, blue and green to zero and the alpha to 1 as well which displays a solid red color onto the screen.
We could make this program a little more interesting by animating it.
#ifdef GL_ES precision mediump float; #endif uniform vec2 u_resolution; uniform vec2 u_mouse; uniform float u_time; void main() { vec2 st = vec2(0.0,0.0); st.x = gl_FragCoord.x/u_resolution.x; st.y = gl_FragCoord.y/u_resolution.y; gl_FragColor = vec4(st.x,st.y,abs(sin(u_time)),1.0); }  After adding in the sin wave to modulate the green channel over time, we get this sort of transitioning animation: Here, gl_FragCoord is another reserved variable which stores the coordinates of each individual screen pixel and is usually the output of a vertex shader. We divide it by the resolution since we want to obtain colour values between 0 and 1 as you can see in the final line of the program, this process is called normalization and the coordinates that range from 0 to 1 are called normalized coordinates. We are altering the green channel with the help of a sin() function which results in the transitioning effect.
Some Examples So far we have seen the absolute baby basics of how shaders work and what they are. When you further add in layers of complexity and model how the pixels behave, you can come up with some impressive pattern like structures which we shall explore sometime in the future. You can plot precise mathematical formulas to draw curved surfaces or use a color shift to enhance the image quality or add certain effects to the display, such as a Gaussian blur. Shaders can also be used to manipulate lighting conditions or add shadows to a scene, which involves some knowledge of physics to a certain extent. Shaders are used extensively in video games, animated movies, vehicle renders, etc. if we’re looking at the most common use case. Basically if you need to render a complex model onto the screen, you’re most likely to use a shader.
  The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild uses a Cel shader     Pixar surface, a surface shader applied on Hank from Finding Dory.   They do tend to get increasingly complex as you keep on adding more details but once you get a good grasp on the ideas of space, time and colours, it’ll be a fun place to let your imagination flow. I shall sign off here and hope to cover some more shader stuff in the future, possibly covering pattern drawings with an example of maybe Truchet tiling in the future. In the meantime you can have a look at some of these wonderful works of art created using shaders.

  Seascape by TDM     Fractal Land by Kali     Rainier mood by Zavie     Noise Contour by candycat     Mandelbulb by EvilRyu     Fractal pyramid by bradjamesgrant     Selfie girl by iq     Perspex Web Lattice by Shane    Links for the above images:  Seascape: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/Ms2SD1 Fractal Land: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/XsBXWt Rainier mood: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/ldfyzl Noise Contour: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/MscSzf Mandelbulb: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/MdXSWn Fractal pyramid: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/tsXBzS Selfie girl: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/WsSBzh Perspex Web Lattice: https://www.shadertoy.com/view/Mld3Rn  References:  https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Games/Techniques/3D_on_the_web/GLSL_Shaders https://www.cs.vu.nl/~eliens/download/literatuur-shaders.pdf http://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs4620/2014fa/lectures/glsl1.pdf

title: A brief introduction to Shaders
